title: The effect of loss/ gain framing and motivational factors on sports engagement.
abstract: The current study aims to examine how framing (loss/gain) and motivation (health/beauty-related) impact individuals' inclination to participate in sports activities. The focus is on understanding how different frames, which highlight either potential losses or gains concerning health and beauty appeals, influence the willingness to engage in sports. Key variables of interest include participants' readiness to undertake sports activities, gauged through self-reported likelihood of involvement in various sports under different framing conditions. The analysis will investigate how framing and motivation interact to affect the willingness to engage in sports activities, while controlling for potential confounders like demographic variables and previous exercise behaviors. This research seeks to enhance our understanding of how framing and motivational factors shape decision-making regarding health and sports involvement. The findings could inform the development of interventions and messages aimed at promoting physical activity and healthy lifestyle choices across diverse populations.

title: Loss vs. gain framing in vaccine allocation decisions
abstract: Vaccine allocations are studied using binary choices, where alternatives represent different allocations of four vaccine doses among 10 people of different age groups (elderly, middle-aged, young). We study allocation choices and the consequently expected expected years of life saved with the vaccine.

title: Promoting sustainable agricultural innovations – investigating the effect of loss and gain framing in videos on implicit and explicit attitudes as well as purchase intention
abstract: This study is designed to investigate the effect of loss and gain framing in videos influencing consumers’ implicit and explicit attitudes towards and purchase intentions of innovations, using the example of beneficial soil microbes in tomato production. Thereby we want to explore whether loss and gain framing with respect to the environmental and health effects of the innovation increases both explicit and implicit attitudes as well as purchase intentions in a positive direction compared to a control video that informs about the innovation. In line with prospect theory (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979), we hypothesize that information about agricultural innovations presented in a loss-frame will induce more positive attitudes (explicit and implicit) towards and higher purchase intentions of an innovation compared to information in a gain frame. Finally, we assume that explicit and implicit attitudes mediate the impact of information on purchase intention.
